does: This paper investigates how fine-tuning audio language models on benign data undermines their safety alignment, a phenomenon previously documented in text and vision but unexplored in audio. The researchers evaluate three state-of-the-art audio LLMs using a proximity-based filtering framework that selects benign audio samples based on their embedding-space distance to harmful content. They decompose this proximity into three distinct dimensions—semantic, acoustic, and mixed—using both external reference encoders and models' internal encoders. The key finding is that benign fine-tuning dramatically increases Jailbreak Success Rate from single digits to as high as 87.12%, with acoustic similarity to harmful content playing a previously underappreciated role in enabling this vulnerability.
connects: This work extends prior findings on alignment degradation through benign fine-tuning in text and vision modalities, now to the audio domain. It builds on research showing that representation-space proximity to harmful content predicts which samples most damage safety, borrowing the methodology of proximity-based analysis. The paper addresses a limitation in existing work by recognizing that embedding-space proximity has been treated monolithically, without decomposing multiple sources of similarity. By introducing acoustic properties alongside semantic content, it responds to the unique structural richness of audio data that prior single-modality studies could not capture, connecting to broader work on multimodal safety vulnerabilities and representation analysis in language models.
enables: This research opens several future directions for audio AI safety. It suggests that decomposing proximity along multiple axes may improve our understanding of alignment vulnerabilities in other multimodal settings, potentially informing defenses that target specific similarity dimensions. The finding that acoustic features drive alignment breakdown points toward new evaluation and mitigation strategies specifically for audio, such as acoustic robustness training or representation-space filtering during fine-tuning. The work enables more informed design of audio LLM architectures and training procedures, and highlights the need for safety frameworks that account for non-semantic modality-specific properties when deploying LLMs across different input types.
